primary_diagnosis.name: Acute pancreatitis (alcohol-induced) with early hypovolaemia and SIRS risk

pathophysiology_summary: Binge alcohol triggers premature intra-acinar activation of pancreatic proenzymes (trypsinogen to trypsin), causing pancreatic autodigestion and an intense local and systemic inflammatory response. Mediator release drives capillary leak and third-spacing of fluid, which — compounded by profuse vomiting and inability to tolerate oral intake — produces intravascular volume depletion, tachycardia and early hypovolaemia. The retroperitoneal location of the inflamed pancreas explains the boring epigastric pain that radiates through to the back and is eased by sitting forward (reducing tension on the retroperitoneum) and worsened by lying supine. A reactive ileus produces vomiting and absent bowel sounds. Without prompt IV fluid resuscitation the trajectory is toward SIRS, acute kidney injury, shock and, in severe cases, necrotising pancreatitis and multi-organ dysfunction. Diagnosis is confirmed by serum lipase/amylase greater than three times the upper limit of normal plus consistent clinical features; severity and complications are assessed with bloods and CT at hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe boring epigastric pain radiating to the back with profuse vomiting is the classic constellation.
Sudden severe epigastric pain can be a perforated viscus; would expect board-like rigidity and peritonism.
Upper-abdominal pain with vomiting; usually right-upper-quadrant and Murphy-positive rather than central-to-back.
Epigastric pain with vomiting can be an atypical inferior MI; must be actively excluded, especially remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold pancreatitis as leading but keep perforation and cardiac mimics live — premature closure here is the key trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Positional relief on leaning forward, recent heavy alcohol, and >10 episodes of bilious vomiting strongly favour pancreatitis.
Still possible; positional relief and absence of sudden board-like rigidity make it less likely but it cannot be excluded remotely.
Positional and pleuritic character argue against ischaemia, but an ECG and troponin are required to exclude it and cannot be done via chat.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Positional relief plus alcohol shifts the balance decisively to pancreatitis, but the AI should not falsely reassure on the cardiac/perforation mimics without hospital workup.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic clinical picture plus dehydration signs; confirmation and severity grading require serum lipase, bloods and imaging at hospital — hence urgent ED transfer.
Retained on the sealed list as a can't-miss that the ED assessment (erect CXR/CT) will exclude; pain spreading across the whole abdomen would raise it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The telehealth-correct endpoint is a presumptive diagnosis with mandatory urgent ED transfer for bloods/imaging — not a confirmed home diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting with inability to tolerate any oral fluids for ~24 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing fluid loss with no replacement drives hypovolaemia; oral rehydration will fail and delays needed IV resuscitation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration, acute kidney injury and hypovolaemic shock.

red_flags.description: Signs of early hypovolaemia — weakness, lethargy, dry mucous membranes, pale appearance (tachycardia and relative hypotension on clinician measurement)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant intravascular volume depletion requiring IV fluid resuscitation, not achievable at home.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised shock; renal failure and multi-organ dysfunction.

red_flags.description: Severe unremitting epigastric pain radiating to the back, unrelieved by OTC analgesia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks a serious intra-abdominal process needing urgent assessment and confirmatory bloods/imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed severe pancreatitis or a perforated viscus.

red_flags.description: Low-grade fever with severe abdominal pain — possible early systemic inflammatory response
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever plus tachycardia raises SIRS; escalates urgency and mandates hospital observation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage of an evolving systemic inflammatory response or infected necrosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing profuse vomiting as viral gastroenteritis and advising oral rehydration at home, missing a severe intra-abdominal emergency.
Failing to consider atypical inferior MI in a patient with epigastric pain and vomiting — it cannot be excluded via chat and warrants ED assessment.
Telling a patient who cannot keep water down to 'sip fluids' — this fails, stimulates the pancreas, and delays IV resuscitation.
Reaching for an NSAID for analgesia in a dehydrated, volume-depleted patient risks precipitating acute kidney injury.
Advising the patient to wait for a morning clinic; the source note explicitly judged that delay carried an unacceptable risk of dehydration, AKI and shock.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: At least 2 of 4 likely met (HR >90, RR >20, temp abnormal) — SIRS-positive
risk_scores.clinical_implication: SIRS at presentation predicts more severe pancreatitis and supports urgent hospital assessment and fluid resuscitation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and heart rate (measured BP 100/65, HR 120)
Temperature to a calibrated thermometer (37.9 degrees C)
Abdominal palpation — epigastric tenderness, voluntary guarding, distension
Auscultation — absent bowel sounds indicating reactive ileus
Serum lipase/amylase (>3x upper limit of normal) and other bloods
Assessment for Cullen's/Grey Turner's signs and for jaundice
CT imaging for severity grading and complications
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe abdominal-pain presentation with dehydration and arrange urgent transfer to the Emergency Department (relative can drive, or 000 if deteriorating) for IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia, antiemesis and confirmatory bloods; keep the patient strictly nil-by-mouth in the meantime.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the pancreatitis picture is classic, but the AI must not confirm it remotely, must keep perforation and cardiac mimics live, and must not be falsely reassured out of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: IAP/APA evidence-based guidelines for the management of acute pancreatitis (2013)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Early goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation and diagnosis by lipase/amylase >3x ULN plus clinical features; admit for monitoring.
Diagnosis requires 2 of 3: characteristic pain, enzymes >3x ULN, or characteristic imaging; severity is graded on organ failure and complications.
